hypotheses.claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) is substantially artifactual: word-final glyphs drawn from the set {y, n, l, r, o} function as grammatical suffixes, and stripping them would reduce the effective vocabulary by at least 30%, collapsing hapax rate to below 50%.
evidence_for: Word-final glyph constraints show {y,n,l,r,o} account for the vast majority of word endings, consistent with a small set of suffix tokens
Top frequent words like 'daiin', 'aiin', 'chedy', 'shedy' share common endings suggesting paradigmatic inflection
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is below 1.0, consistent with a vocabulary inflated by suffix variants of stem words
evidence_against: If suffixes were purely grammatical, we would expect cleaner paradigm clusters around common stems
Hapax rate of 70.1% is extremely high even for inflected languages with suffixes stripped
Entropy of 3.8627 bits is close to natural language levels, not obviously inflated by suffix tokens
hypotheses.test: Compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio after stripping all word-final characters belonging to {y, n, l, r, o}; compare suffix-stripped Zipf exponent and type-token ratio to baseline
hypotheses.test_metric: Hapax ratio and vocabulary size of suffix-stripped corpus vs. original
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if hapax ratio drops below 50% after stripping; denial if hapax ratio remains above 60%
tests_remaining: Compute vocabulary size and hapax ratio after stripping all word-final characters belonging to {y, n, l, r, o}; compare suffix-stripped Zipf exponent and type-token ratio to baseline
hypotheses.reasoning: If suffixes account for most lexical diversity, confirming this would collapse a major anomaly and strongly constrain the cipher model toward an inflected-language substitution scheme

hypotheses.claim: The Currier A and Currier B sub-corpora use two structurally distinct cipher tables encoding the same underlying Latin plaintext, evidenced by differing word-initial glyph frequency distributions between the two sub-corpora that are nonetheless both consistent with Latin word-onset phoneme distributions.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show measurable statistical differences suggesting different encoding schemes
Current evidence at 0.426 probability supports two distinct cipher tables for A and B

If the same Latin text were encoded with two different substitution tables, both tables would independently map Latin onset consonants/vowels to Voynich glyphs, producing correlated but shifted frequency profiles
evidence_against: Currier A/B split may reflect scribal hand differences rather than cipher-table differences
Evidence at 0.677 supports a single underlying language encoded by two scribes rather than two cipher tables
Shared high-frequency words across A and B sections suggest significant cipher overlap rather than complete table separation
hypotheses.test: Compute word-initial glyph frequency vectors for Currier A and B separately; run chi-squared test for independence; then test whether the two frequency vectors are consistent with a common Latin onset-frequency profile under two different permutation mappings
hypotheses.test_metric: Chi-squared statistic on word-initial glyph distributions A vs. B; cosine similarity of each distribution to permuted Latin phoneme-onset frequencies
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if chi-squared p < 0.01 (distributions differ) AND both sub-corpora independently achieve cosine similarity > 0.7 with some permutation of Latin onset frequencies; denial if distributions are not significantly different or neither maps to Latin
tests_remaining: Compute word-initial glyph frequency vectors for Currier A and B separately; run chi-squared test for independence; then test whether the two frequency vectors are consistent with a common Latin onset-frequency profile under two different permutation mappings
hypotheses.reasoning: This directly distinguishes between the two leading explanations for the A/B split (two scribes vs. two cipher tables) and would either unify or further bifurcate the solution space

hypotheses.claim: The zodiac section's anomalously low entropy (3.7149 bits, lowest of all sections) reflects a label-heavy encoding in which a large fraction of tokens are proper-name labels (star names, month names) encoded with a homophonic cipher, producing reduced glyph entropy relative to prose sections.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has lowest entropy of all sections (3.7149 bits) and only 1,297 words across 12 folios, consistent with sparse label text rather than continuous prose
Astronomical section also shows low entropy (3.7471 bits) and similarly sparse word count (872 words, 8 folios), consistent with label-dominated content
Homophonic substitution of a small fixed label vocabulary would reduce effective glyph entropy by concentrating probability mass on fewer glyph sequences
evidence_against: Low entropy could alternatively reflect a simpler or less redundant cipher rather than label-specific encoding
If zodiac labels encoded known medieval star/month names, partial decipherment via frequency matching would already have succeeded
Word length distribution in zodiac section would need to match typical medieval Latin star-name lengths (3-8 characters)
hypotheses.test: Compute type-token ratio and average word length distribution for zodiac and astronomical sections vs. herbal and recipes sections; test whether zodiac/astronomical show significantly higher type-token ratio (fewer repeated tokens) consistent with label lists, or lower (consistent with repeated formulaic labels)
hypotheses.test_metric: Type-token ratio per section, mean and variance of word length per section, proportion of hapax legomena per section
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if zodiac type-token ratio is significantly lower than herbal/recipes (p < 0.05), indicating repeated labels; denial if zodiac hapax rate matches or exceeds herbal section hapax rate
tests_remaining: Compute type-token ratio and average word length distribution for zodiac and astronomical sections vs. herbal and recipes sections; test whether zodiac/astronomical show significantly higher type-token ratio (fewer repeated tokens) consistent with label lists, or lower (consistent with repeated formulaic labels)
hypotheses.reasoning: Label-dominated sections would exhibit a characteristic statistical fingerprint distinct from prose; confirming this would justify targeted decipherment attempts using known medieval astronomical nomenclature as a crib

hypotheses.claim: The text-only section (7 folios, entropy 3.9016 bits, highest of all sections) represents unenciphered or lightly enciphered natural language prose, while all other sections apply an additional transposition or homophonic layer on top of a base substitution cipher, producing their characteristically lower entropy.
evidence_for: Text-only section entropy (3.9016 bits) is closest to natural Latin (~4.0 bits) and Italian (~3.95 bits) of any section
A secondary cipher layer applied on top of a substitution cipher would reduce entropy by spreading probability mass, consistent with all other sections showing lower entropy than text-only
If text-only is 'base' cipher and other sections are double-enciphered, the entropy gradient across sections is directly predicted
evidence_against: Alternative explanation: text-only simply contains more linguistically varied content (prose vs. formulaic plant descriptions) that naturally yields higher entropy without any cipher difference
Entropy difference between text-only (3.9016) and recipes (3.8586) is only ~0.04 bits, which may not be statistically significant given small sample size (2,349 words)
No external evidence confirms the text-only section was produced by a different encipherment process
hypotheses.test: Run bootstrap resampling to establish 95% confidence intervals on entropy estimates for each section; test whether text-only entropy is significantly different from all other sections; then test whether the entropy ordering (text-only > recipes > cosmological > herbal > biological > pharmaceutical > astronomical > zodiac) is consistent with a monotonic relationship between cipher complexity and entropy reduction
hypotheses.test_metric: Bootstrap confidence intervals on per-section Shannon entropy; Spearman rank correlation between section entropy and hypothesized cipher-layer count
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if text-only CI does not overlap with any other section CI at 95% confidence; denial if text-only entropy CI overlaps with two or more other sections
tests_remaining: Run bootstrap resampling to establish 95% confidence intervals on entropy estimates for each section; test whether text-only entropy is significantly different from all other sections; then test whether the entropy ordering (text-only > recipes > cosmological > herbal > biological > pharmaceutical > astronomical > zodiac) is consistent with a monotonic relationship between cipher complexity and entropy reduction
hypotheses.reasoning: If text-only is statistically distinguishable from all other sections in entropy, it strongly motivates treating it as a different cipher tier and using it as a privileged target for initial decipherment attempts

hypotheses.claim: The dominant word-initial glyph constraint (o, c, q, s, d accounting for most word starts) and word-final constraint (y, n, l, r, o) reflect a Vigenere-type polyalphabetic cipher in which the cipher alphabet at word boundaries is fixed, causing structural glyph repetition that is unrelated to plaintext phoneme distribution at those positions.
evidence_for: A Vigenere cipher with key reset at word boundaries would mechanically produce consistent word-initial and word-final glyph distributions regardless of plaintext content
Positional constraints are strong enough (current evidence 0.605-0.639 probability) to suggest a cipher-structural rather than grammatical origin
Word-boundary key reset was a documented technique in period cipher construction to aid the encipherer in tracking key position
evidence_against: Vigenere-type ciphers typically produce higher entropy than observed (3.86 bits); a word-boundary-reset Vigenere on Latin/Italian should approach 4.0 bits unless key is very short
Short Vigenere keys are vulnerable to Kasiski analysis; no strong Kasiski periodicity has been confirmed in the Voynich corpus
The specific glyph set {o,c,q,s,d} at word-initial positions may reflect plaintext vowel/consonant onset distribution rather than a fixed cipher alphabet
hypotheses.test: Apply Kasiski test and Index of Coincidence analysis to the full corpus and to Currier A and B sub-corpora separately; compute autocorrelation of glyph sequences at word-internal positions to detect periodic key cycling; compare IoC values to expected values for Vigenere with key lengths 2-10
hypotheses.test_metric: Index of Coincidence for word-internal glyph sequences; Kasiski distance distribution; autocorrelation coefficient at lags 1-10 within word-internal positions
hypotheses.test_threshold: Confirmation if IoC at word-internal positions significantly exceeds IoC at word-boundary positions AND Kasiski test identifies repeating distance factors consistent with key length 2-6; denial if IoC is uniform across word positions or no Kasiski periodicity is found
tests_remaining: Apply Kasiski test and Index of Coincidence analysis to the full corpus and to Currier A and B sub-corpora separately; compute autocorrelation of glyph sequences at word-internal positions to detect periodic key cycling; compare IoC values to expected values for Vigenere with key lengths 2-10
hypotheses.reasoning: A word-boundary-reset polyalphabetic cipher is a mechanistically plausible period technique that would explain positional constraints while potentially being breakable via standard polyalphabetic cryptanalysis methods applied word-internally
